questions.questionText: The principal sites of dietary carbohydrate digestion are:
questions.explanation: Dietary carbohydrate digestion mainly occurs in the mouth and intestinal lumen. The goal is to break glycosidic bonds and produce absorbable monosaccharides.

questions.questionText: Carbohydrate digestion is catalyzed mainly by:
questions.explanation: Glycosidases hydrolyze glycosidic bonds between carbohydrate units. This converts dietary carbohydrates into smaller sugars and finally into monosaccharides.

questions.questionText: The final absorbable products of carbohydrate digestion include:
questions.explanation: The final products of carbohydrate digestion are monosaccharides: glucose, galactose, and fructose. These are absorbed by cells of the small intestine.

questions.questionText: Metabolism is best defined as:
options: The sum of all chemical changes occurring in the cell, tissue, or body
questions.explanation: Metabolism includes all chemical reactions in living cells. It includes both breakdown pathways that release energy and synthetic pathways that build complex molecules.

questions.questionText: In a metabolic pathway:
options: The product of one reaction becomes the substrate of the next reaction
Every reaction occurs completely isolated from the others
questions.explanation: Metabolic pathways are multistep sequences. The product formed in one step is used as the substrate for the following step, creating an organized flow of metabolites.

questions.questionText: Catabolism is generally described as:
questions.explanation: Catabolism breaks complex nutrients into simpler products. Many different fuels converge into common end products or intermediates, so catabolism is considered convergent.

questions.questionText: Anabolism is generally described as:
questions.explanation: Anabolism builds complex molecules from simpler precursors. From a limited number of precursors, many different complex molecules can be formed, so anabolism is divergent.

questions.questionText: Cycles are metabolic pathways that:
questions.explanation: A cycle is a pathway in which a component is regenerated as part of the sequence. This allows the pathway to continue repeatedly.

questions.questionText: Glycolysis is:
options: Oxidation of glucose or glycogen to pyruvate or lactate
Formation of glucose from non-carbohydrate sources
Oxidation of acetyl-CoA in the TCA cycle only
questions.explanation: Glycolysis breaks down glucose or glycogen for energy production. Under aerobic conditions, pyruvate is produced; under anaerobic conditions, lactate is produced.

questions.questionText: Under aerobic conditions, glycolysis yields:
questions.explanation: Aerobic glycolysis converts one glucose molecule into two molecules of pyruvate. Pyruvate can then enter mitochondria for further oxidation.

questions.questionText: Under anaerobic conditions, glycolysis yields:
questions.explanation: In anaerobic conditions, pyruvate is reduced to lactate. This is important in exercising muscle and red blood cells.

questions.questionText: All enzymes of glycolysis are located in the:
questions.explanation: All enzymes of glycolysis are found in the cytoplasm. This is why glycolysis can occur even in cells lacking mitochondria, such as mature RBCs.

questions.questionText: Most glycolytic reactions are:
questions.explanation: Most reactions of glycolysis are reversible. However, three key reactions are irreversible and serve as important regulatory points.

questions.questionText: The irreversible steps of glycolysis are catalyzed by:
questions.explanation: Glycolysis has three irreversible steps: glucose phosphorylation, fructose-6-phosphate phosphorylation, and PEP conversion to pyruvate. These are catalyzed by hexokinase/glucokinase, PFK-1, and pyruvate kinase.

questions.questionText: Glycolysis is divided into:
options: Energy investment phase and energy generation phase
questions.explanation: The first phase consumes ATP to prepare glucose for breakdown. The second phase generates ATP and NADH, producing pyruvate or lactate depending on oxygen availability.

questions.questionText: The first phase of glycolysis is called the:
questions.explanation: The first five reactions consume ATP and convert glucose into two molecules of glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate. This phase prepares the molecule for energy generation later.

questions.questionText: The second phase of glycolysis is called the:
questions.explanation: The second phase includes steps 6–10. Two molecules of GAP are converted into pyruvate, generating ATP and NADH.

questions.questionText: The first reaction of glycolysis converts glucose into:
questions.explanation: The first glycolytic step phosphorylates glucose to glucose-6-phosphate. This reaction uses ATP and is catalyzed by hexokinase or glucokinase.

questions.questionText: The first reaction of glycolysis is catalyzed by:
questions.explanation: Glucose phosphorylation is catalyzed by hexokinase in most tissues or glucokinase in the liver. This step is irreversible.

questions.questionText: The glucose phosphorylation reaction requires:
questions.explanation: The first step of glycolysis uses ATP as phosphate donor and energy source. Mg²⁺ is required for kinase activity.

questions.questionText: Phosphorylation of glucose traps it inside the cell because:
options: Glucose-6-phosphate is too polar and lacks transmembrane carriers
Glucose-6-phosphate immediately crosses the membrane faster
Glucose-6-phosphate is converted into lactate outside the cell
questions.explanation: Phosphorylated sugars cannot easily diffuse through the lipid core of membranes. Also, there are no transmembrane carriers for these phosphorylated compounds, so glucose is trapped as G6P.

questions.questionText: The second step of glycolysis converts:
questions.explanation: Phosphohexose isomerase converts G6P, an aldose sugar phosphate, into F6P, a ketose sugar phosphate. This reaction is reversible.

questions.questionText: The enzyme of step 2 of glycolysis is:
questions.explanation: Phosphohexose isomerase catalyzes the reversible conversion of glucose-6-phosphate to fructose-6-phosphate.

questions.questionText: The third step of glycolysis converts fructose-6-phosphate into:
questions.explanation: PFK-1 phosphorylates fructose-6-phosphate using ATP to produce fructose-1,6-bisphosphate. This is an irreversible and highly regulated step.

questions.questionText: The third step of glycolysis is catalyzed by:
questions.explanation: PFK-1 catalyzes phosphorylation of fructose-6-phosphate to fructose-1,6-bisphosphate. It is the main rate-limiting enzyme of glycolysis.

questions.questionText: The main rate-limiting step of glycolysis is catalyzed by:
questions.explanation: PFK-1 is the principal regulatory and rate-limiting enzyme of glycolysis. It responds strongly to cellular energy status.

questions.questionText: PFK-1 is inhibited by:
questions.explanation: High ATP indicates abundant energy, so glycolysis slows. Citrate also signals energy sufficiency and inhibits PFK-1.

questions.questionText: PFK-1 is activated by:
questions.explanation: AMP signals low cellular energy and activates glycolysis. Fructose-2,6-bisphosphate is the most potent activator of PFK-1.

questions.questionText: Fructose-2,6-bisphosphate is formed by:
questions.explanation: Fructose-2,6-bisphosphate is produced by PFK-2, which is different from PFK-1. PFK-1 uses fructose-6-phosphate to form fructose-1,6-bisphosphate.

questions.questionText: Fructose-2,6-bisphosphate:
options: Activates both pathways fully at the same time
Has no role in reciprocal regulation
questions.explanation: Fructose-2,6-bisphosphate activates PFK-1 and inhibits fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase. This prevents glycolysis and gluconeogenesis from being fully active simultaneously.

questions.questionText: The fourth step of glycolysis cleaves fructose-1,6-bisphosphate into:
questions.explanation: Aldolase cleaves fructose-1,6-bisphosphate into two triose phosphates: dihydroxyacetone phosphate and glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate.

questions.questionText: The enzyme that cleaves fructose-1,6-bisphosphate is:
questions.explanation: Aldolase catalyzes the reversible cleavage of fructose-1,6-bisphosphate into DHAP and GAP.

questions.questionText: DHAP is converted into GAP by:
questions.explanation: Phosphotriose isomerase converts DHAP into GAP. This allows both three-carbon fragments from glucose to continue through the same payoff pathway.

questions.questionText: After the first five steps of glycolysis, the net product is:
questions.explanation: Phase one converts one glucose molecule into two molecules of GAP. These two GAP molecules proceed through the energy-generating phase.

questions.questionText: The first oxidation-reduction reaction of glycolysis is catalyzed by:
questions.explanation: GAP dehydrogenase oxidizes glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate to 1,3-bisphosphoglycerate and reduces NAD⁺ to NADH.

questions.questionText: In the GAP dehydrogenase step, NAD⁺ is converted into:
questions.explanation: GAP is oxidized, while NAD⁺ is reduced to NADH + H⁺. This is the first redox step of glycolysis.

questions.questionText: The first substrate-level phosphorylation in glycolysis occurs during conversion of:
questions.explanation: Phosphoglycerate kinase transfers phosphate from 1,3-BPG to ADP, forming ATP. This is the first ATP-producing step in glycolysis.

questions.questionText: The enzyme of the first ATP-forming step in glycolysis is:
questions.explanation: Phosphoglycerate kinase catalyzes ATP formation from 1,3-BPG and ADP. Because two GAP molecules are processed, this step produces two ATP per glucose.

questions.questionText: The conversion of 3-phosphoglycerate to 2-phosphoglycerate is catalyzed by:
questions.explanation: Phosphoglycerate mutase shifts the phosphate group from carbon 3 to carbon 2, producing 2-phosphoglycerate.

questions.questionText: Enolase converts:
questions.explanation: Enolase dehydrates 2-phosphoglycerate to form phosphoenolpyruvate. This step is reversible and produces a high-energy phosphate compound.

questions.questionText: Fluoride inhibits glycolysis by inhibiting:
questions.explanation: Fluoride inhibits enolase. This is why fluoride is added to blood samples to inhibit glycolysis before blood glucose estimation.

questions.questionText: The last step of glycolysis converts PEP into:
questions.explanation: Pyruvate kinase converts phosphoenolpyruvate into pyruvate while producing ATP. This is the second substrate-level phosphorylation step.

questions.questionText: The last step of glycolysis is catalyzed by:
questions.explanation: Pyruvate kinase catalyzes the irreversible conversion of PEP to pyruvate with ATP production.

questions.questionText: Under anaerobic conditions, pyruvate is reduced to lactate by:
questions.explanation: Lactate dehydrogenase converts pyruvate to lactate. This regenerates NAD⁺ so glycolysis can continue in anaerobic conditions.

questions.questionText: Oxidative decarboxylation of pyruvate converts pyruvate into:
questions.explanation: Pyruvate dehydrogenase complex converts pyruvate into acetyl-CoA inside mitochondria. This reaction links glycolysis to the citric acid cycle.
